items.q: Which mood follows 'quiero que' (I want that)?
items.why: Students use indicative after que. Expressions of wish, emotion, doubt, or impersonal expressions trigger SUBJUNCTIVE: 'Quiero que VENGAS' (not 'que vienes'). Different subjects require que + subjunctive.

items.q: Why does 'inglés' have a written accent?
items.correct: It stresses the final syllable while ending in -s, which is unusual.
items.misconception: All Spanish words have accent marks.
items.why: Students treat accents as random. They follow STRESS RULES: words stressed normally don't need accents; the accent shows when stress falls outside the default position (or distinguishes homophones).

items.q: How do you say 'I don't see anything' in Spanish?
items.correct: No veo nada (double negative is correct).
items.why: Students avoid 'double negatives' as in English. Spanish REQUIRES them when nada/nadie/nunca follows the verb: 'no...nada', 'no...nadie', 'no...nunca'. Only one negative if it precedes ('Nada veo').
It's Spain. “Mexico” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's Redes sociales. “Medios sociales” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was El Pais founded?
items.why: The real figure is 1976. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
